infons.title: KEYWORDS

passages.text: Advancing nanomedicine with machine learning: predicting protein corona and nano-bio interactions
ABSTRACT

The protein corona (PC) has widely been demonstrated to impact the pharmacokinetics, safety, and efficiency of nanoparticle (NP) systems for clinical applications such as drug or vaccine delivery. However, a comprehensive understanding of PC formation and its overall impact on biological behavior remains challenging due to the wide range of possible physicochemical and experimental parameters that may impact NP-PC formation and interactions, leaving important patterns difficult to identify. Machine learning (ML) algorithms have increasingly been used to analyze datasets and identify patterns that govern NP-PC interactions, showing great potential to enhance NP-PC experimental and pharmacological analysis. In this review, we conducted a systemic literature review for ML-based NP-PC analyses in PubMed and Web of Science between 1 January 2000 to 1 November 2025. We discuss key developments in the identified ML workflows for the prediction of NP-PC interactions, including PC composition and formation dynamics as well as pharmacological and toxicologically relevant endpoints like NP biodistribution and cellular uptake. We also highlight future perspectives in the field, such as improving dataset diversity, analytical protocol harmonization, and model transparency. We aim to guide future research toward more robust and informative ML approaches for optimizing NP design and predicting nano-bio interactions.
Plain Language Summary
Using Machine Learning to Understand Nanoparticle Behavior in Body

When a nanoparticle enters as the body blood, they quickly become covered by proteins found in fluids like blood. This layer of proteins is referred to as a “protein corona.” Protein coronas can change how the nanoparticle acts, including how well they deliver drugs or detect disease. For this reason, predicting what proteins will coat nanoparticle surfaces is important for designing safe and effective nanoparticles. However, this is difficult because many factors can change the outcome, such as the size and shape of the nanoparticle, the types of proteins present, and the conditions in the body. Machine learning is recently being used to find patterns in large, complex data, helping researchers to understand how protein coronas form and how nanoparticles behave in biological systems. In this review, we explain recent progress in using machine learning to study these interactions. We also discuss current challenges, such as limited or inconsistent data, and suggest ways to improve predictions. Better predictions could help researchers design safer and more effective nanomedicines in the future.
Introduction

Nanomedicine has emerged in recent years as a revolutionary approach in drug delivery and diagnostics, leveraging the unique properties of engineered nanoparticles (NPs) to enhance the safety and efficacy of small-molecule drugs, vaccines, diagnostic sensing and imaging agents, and other therapeutic agents. However, clinical application of NP systems remains challenging. This is in part due to the rapid adsorption of proteins onto NP surfaces when introduced into biological environments, forming what is known as the protein corona (PC). PC formation has widely been observed to alter NP physicochemical properties, including surface characteristics, effective size, and colloidal stability, which in turn modulate nano-bio interactions and downstream biological behavior. NP-PC interactions can ultimately pharmacokinetics, cellular targeting and uptake efficiency, and off-target or toxic interactions. As such, understanding the impacts of the PC and the parameters in NP formulation and experimental methodology that promote or inhibit protein adsorption is highly desirable to minimize risk of unintended interactions and potentially exploit the PC for enhanced therapeutic efficiency.

Machine learning (ML)-guided framework for NP–PC analysis and design optimization. NP properties determine PC formation in biological fluids, which subsequently modulates NP bioactivity. Datasets combining NP descriptors, PC-derived descriptors, and/or biological assay results can be integrated using ML models to identify structure–PC–function relationships and predict biological outcomes. Model predictions enable prioritization of NP formulations for experimental validation, forming an iterative active-learning loop that accelerates the rational design of nanomedicines. Created with BioRender.com.

Nanoparticle design parameters include material, morphology, surface coating or ligands and surface chemistry. Protein corona formation involves adsorption behavior and protein composition. Biological responses include pharmacokinetics, toxicity and immune response and therapeutic efficiency. Machine learning modeling integrates data from nanoparticle descriptors, protein descriptors and biological assays using algorithms like random forest, gradient boosting, neural network and t-SNE. Model outputs predict protein binding, bioactivity and identify important features. Guided optimization involves top formulations and experimental prioritization through in vitro validation, in vivo testing and proteomics validation. An iterative active learning loop is indicated for continuous improvement.
Diagram of nanoparticle design, protein corona formation and biological responses using machine learning for optimization.

Subtle changes in NP design and physicochemical properties, biological environment, and even experimental measurement techniques have been demonstrated to impact the observed PC, but systematically studying all variables and their potential interactions is often a costly and time-consuming process. These limitations have motivated the growing interest in computational strategies, namely machine learning (ML) algorithms, to complement experimental NP-PC analysis. By learning complex patterns that relate NP physicochemical properties, protein characteristics, and experimental conditions to outcomes such as protein adsorption profiles, PC composition, and downstream biological effects, ML models can capture the non-linear and high-dimensional nature of NP-PC systems. These data-driven approaches support a powerful framework for elucidating mechanistic insights into protein adsorption and protein-bio interactions that can guide experimental prioritization and inform rational NP design to optimize therapeutic efficacy and safety (Figure 1). Several recent reviews have summarized the application of machine learning (ML) methods in nanomedicine and NP-PC research, highlighting emerging predictive models, their potential applications and key insights into proteins features found to be strongly correlated with desired NP characteristics or biological endpoints. Building on these contributions, there is also value in examining how predictive models are constructed, validated, and applied across the literature, as methodological choices can substantially influence model performance, interpretability, and generalizability. In particular, aspects such as modeling frameworks, dataset composition, feature engineering strategies, and reporting practices play important roles in determining how reliably ML approaches can capture nano-bio interactions and how readily models can be adapted to new nanoparticle systems or biological contexts.

In this review, we provide a comprehensive overview of recent ML-based approaches used to study NP-PC interactions while focusing specifically on how predictive models are constructed, validated, and applied across the literature. Through a systematic survey of published studies, we examine how researchers have used ML models to predict PC composition, investigate NP-protein interaction properties, and leverage PC-derived features to improve prediction of downstream biological outcomes. We analyze emerging trends in dataset composition, feature selection strategies, and model development practices that influence predictive performance. We also highlight key limitations in current datasets, including limited nanoparticle diversity, small sample sizes, and experimental variability, which constrain the development of robust and generalizable models and provide suggestions for addressing these limitations.
Workflow of ML for prediction protein corona and nano-bio interactions

Schematic of a typical ML pipeline used to develop and validate predictive models of NP-PC interactions. (1) Data curation and preprocessing: Raw features are processed into model-ready formats and split into training/validation and independent test sets. (2) Model development and evaluation: Models are trained using cross-validation with iterative hyperparameter tuning and feature selection; performance metrics are averaged across folds, and the final model is evaluated on the held-out test set. (3) Results interpretation: Post-hoc methods are applied to interpret model behavior and data structure. (4) External validation: The trained model is applied to independent experimental datasets and predictions are compared with experimental outcomes to assess real-world robustness. Created in BioRender. Canchola, A. (2026) https://BioRender.com/ym2wk5k.

The flowchart illustrates a machine learning pipeline for nanoparticle-protein corona interactions. It consists of four main stages: 1. Data Curation and Pre-Processing: Raw features from nanoparticle descriptors, protein descriptors and experimental assays/methods undergo filtering, normalization, feature encoding and data transformations. The dataset is split into training, validation and test sets. 2. Model Development and Evaluation: Training involves cross-validation with hyperparameter search and feature selection. K-fold cross-validation is depicted with multiple folds. Evaluation includes average performance across folds, standard deviation and final model assessment. Classification metrics include AUC, accuracy and F1-score; regression metrics include R squared, RMSE and MSE. 3. Interpretation: Clustering and feature importance analysis can be used to interpret results. 4. External Validation: The trained model is applied to new datasets, including proteomics/protein interaction and bioactivity assays, to re-evaluate model performance.
Flowchart of a typical ML pipeline for NP-PC interactions: data curation, model development and evaluation, interpretation, and external validation.

ML in nanomedicine has progressed from composition-only models to frameworks that jointly predict PC composition and downstream nano-bio interactions. Across studies, a practical pipeline comprises (1) data curation and pre-processing, (2) model development and evaluation, (3) results interpretation, and (5) external validation (Figure 2). The central objective is to learn relationships among NP descriptors, protein/sequence/structure features, and experimental context to predict what proteins will bind (PC identity/abundance) and what biological impact they will have (nano-bio outcomes). A glossary for key ML concepts can be found in Table S1.
Data curation and pre-processing

The first step, data processing and feature selection, is crucial for PC prediction it provides models with heterogeneous features inputs that capture key NP-PC interaction characteristics. Common inputs are drawn from three domains: NP features typically include material, size, polydispersity index (PDI), surface charge, electrokinetic properties (e.g., zeta potential; ζ-potential), and surface functionalization or coatings, along with other physicochemical descriptors. Protein features include isoelectric point (pI), molecular weight (MW), amino-acid composition and hydropathy indices (e.g., Grand Average of Hydropathicity, GRAVY), intrinsic disorder/secondary-structure proxies, and surface accessibility/3D surrogates; experimental context captures medium/protein source and abundance, ionic strength, pH, incubation time, agitation, and isolation protocols. Depending on the task, quantitative proteomics may serve as inputs or targets.

Prior to modeling, heterogeneous datasets typically require data harmonization and preprocessing. This may include standardizing units and measurement scales across studies, resolving inconsistent nomenclature for proteins or nanoparticle materials, and removing duplicate or inconsistent records. Standard preprocessing steps include: converting categorical variables into numeric representations (e.g., one-hot or label encoding), scaling numeric variables to comparable ranges (e.g., min-max normalization or z-score standardization), and applying log transformations to stabilize highly skewed distributions often observed high dimensional datasets such as proteomics data. Transparent handling of missing values is also important, using documented imputation approaches where appropriate rather than discarding incomplete observations. In proteomics-derived datasets, additional normalization or batch-effect correction may be required to account for differences in measurement platforms or experimental conditions.
Model development and evaluation
Overview of common machine learning (ML) algorithms used for classification or regression endpoints with representative examples identified in the systematic literature search.

Notes: a“Prediction Type”: Specifies whether the model is designed to perform classification, regression, or both types of predictive tasks; b“Representative Examples”: Identifies studies from the resulting literature search that employ this model type.

Next, algorithm and task formulation should be carefully considered. For PC composition, common targets include binary presence/absence, multiclass enriched-depleted-no-change, and continuous relative protein abundance (%-RPA). For nano-bio endpoints, targets span cellular association, biodistribution, and more. Algorithm choice depends on data size, feature complexity, heterogeneity, target output, and the desired accuracy-interpretability balance (Table 1). Strong baselines include linear/logistic models and tree-based ensemble models such as Random Forest (RF) or Gradient Boosting Decision Trees (GBDT) Deep learning is appropriate when data volume and dimensionality support it – for example, convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for nanoparticle morphology, sequence or structure embeddings for protein representations, or graph neural networks for nanocluster or molecular interaction modeling.

Rigorous assessment requires evaluation strategies that minimize bias and ensure that reported performance reflects true generalization to new data. A standard approach is cross-validation (CV), in which the dataset is repeatedly divided into multiple training and testing subsets (“folds”), with model performance averaged across iterations. A more robust variant is study-aware, stratified CV, where splits preserve key dataset properties (e.g., class balance) while keeping samples from the same study or experimental batch grouped together. This prevents closely related samples from appearing in both training and testing sets, reducing bias from batch effects that could otherwise inflate estimates of model accuracy. During model development, feature selection and preprocessing transformations (e.g., scaling, imputation, dimensionality reduction) are best implemented within the training portion of each CV fold, with the learned transformations subsequently applied to the later validation fold. This procedure prevents information leakage and ensures that performance estimates reflect how the model would behave on truly unseen data. Common feature selection approaches include filter methods (e.g., Pearson correlation or ANOVA tests), which evaluate each feature independently; wrapper methods such as recursive feature elimination, which iteratively assess subsets of variables using a trained model; and embedded methods (e.g., LASSO regularization), in which feature selection occurs during model fitting itself. For high-dimensional datasets such as proteomics, dimensionality reduction techniques, including principal component analysis (PCA), linear discriminant analysis (LDA), or uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP), may also be used to summarize correlated features or facilitate exploratory visualization.

Hyperparameter tuning is also commonly used during model development to optimize algorithm performance while reducing the risk of overfitting. Hyperparameters are model settings (e.g., learning rates, tree depth, regularization strength) specified before training. Although these parameters can be manually set or left at default values, systematic tuning is generally preferred because model performance can vary substantially across parameter choices, and default settings are rarely optimal for a specific dataset or prediction task. Tuning methods, such as grid search, random search, or Bayesian optimization, evaluate multiple candidate parameter combinations in a structured manner, allowing the training process to identify configurations that balance model flexibility and generalization performance. In rigorous workflows, hyperparameter tuning is conducted within an inner CV loop, while model evaluation is performed in an outer loop (nested CV) or on a separate validation set.

Finally, trained models should be evaluated using held-out test set (internal validation) or external datasets (external validation) that are not used during training or model selection. common metrics include the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC-ROC), which quantifies the ability of the model to distinguish between classes across decision thresholds, as well as the F1 score, which balances precision (the proportion of predicted positives that are correct) and recall (the proportion of true positives that are correctly detected). When class imbalance is substantial, the area under the precision-recall curve (PR-AUC) can provide a more informative measure of performance.

For regression tasks that predict continuous values, performance is typically summarized using metrics such as the coefficient of determination (R2), which reflects the proportion of variance explained by the model, and error-based measures including root mean squared error (RMSE) and mean absolute error (MAE), which quantify the average magnitude of prediction errors. In scenarios where the objective is to prioritize or rank candidates (for example, identifying the most promising biomarkers or compounds), rank-based metrics that evaluate how well the model orders items by predicted importance may be more appropriate than absolute accuracy measures.
Results interpretation

Post-hoc interpretation methods help translate predictive models into design-relevant insight. Feature importance analysis techniques are commonly used to assign scores to input features based on their contribution to a model’s predictive ability. Common methods include Gini importance or Mean Decrease in Impurity, frequently used with decision tree and RF models, and Shapely Additive exPlanations (SHAP), a model-agnostic framework. Whereas Gini importance estimates a feature’s global importance by summing the reduction in classification error (impurity) produced by splits using that feature across all trees in an ensemble, SHAP analysis quantifies the marginal contribution of each feature to individual model predictions. This enables both local explanations for specific predictions and aggregated global feature importance.

Importantly, these interpretation approaches largely quantify associations learned by the model, rather than causal mechanisms. Feature importance scores, for example, more so reflect a models decision making behavior and the features it mostly strongly relies on to explain variation within the training data, rather than proving that those variables directly drive the underlying biological process. Thus, these analyses are best viewed as hypothesis-generating tools that can guide experimental validation or targeted mechanistic studies. To move beyond purely correlational interpretation, emerging causal probing approaches (e.g., causal-SHAP or counterfactual tests) can help to evaluate whether highly ranked features behave as potential drivers rather than statistical proxies. Although these approaches are still relatively uncommon in NP-PC modeling studies, they represent highly promising practices for improving mechanistic interpretability.
External and prospective validation

Beyond internal CV and model evaluation, external validation using datasets independently collected from a wider diversity of laboratories, experimental protocols, and NP designs is crucial for ensuring that a model is generalizable and transportable across experimental contexts. Several recommended practices can strengthen external validation. First, pre-registered holdouts may be used, wherein experimental data is purposely excluded from model development for later, unbiased evaluations. Second, conducting prospective wet-lab experiments on a subset, typically candidates ranked highest by the model, to confirm predictive accuracy. Third, performing applicability domain checks before inference on novel NPs or experimental conditions to ensure the predictions are reliable and meaningful outside of the initial training data. External validation remains limited in many of the highlighted ML-PC studies in this review, highlighting the need for more scalable validation strategies.

In order to evaluate how NP-PC studies have implemented these techniques, we conducted a systematic literature search in PubMed and Web of Science databases for studies published between 1 January 2000 to 1 November 2025. Articles that conducted ML-based analysis, including predictive and supervised or unsupervised clustering models, on NP-PC relevant systems were included for review. A total of 31 articles were identified. A detailed workflow of the literature search process can be found in Figure S1. In the following sections, we illustrate how these methodological components have been implemented in practice by reviewing representative ML frameworks used to model protein corona formation, NP-PC interactions, and downstream biological effects.
ML-driven protein corona prediction & characterization

ML has been increasingly applied in nanomedicine to predict and optimize NP behavior. Early applications modeled bare NP systems, linking physicochemical properties and biological outcomes such as cytotoxicity, biodistribution, or cellular uptake without explicitly accounting for PC effects. Accumulating evidence now shows that PC formation profoundly alters NP identity and nano-bio interactions, motivating a new generation of ML-driven models that explicitly incorporate protein adsorption dynamics, compositional profiles, and NP-protein interaction mechanisms. This section summarizes representative modeling strategies for direct PC composition prediction (Section 3.1) and indirect NP-PC interaction prediction (Section 3.2).
Predicting PC compositional profiles
Workflow for protein classification models developed by Canchola et al.. Created in BioRender. Canchola, A. (2026) https://BioRender.com/3d8s18h.

Model begins with an 'Input' dataset consisting of 'Nanoparticle Descriptors' and 'Incubation Methods' and 'Target' dataset which includes 'Protein Presence' for APOE, APOB-100, CO3 and CLUS. These inputs lead to the 'Dataset'. The dataset is divided into 'Training' and 'Test' phases. The training phase includes an 'Outer Loop' and an 'Inner Loop'. The outer loop alternates between 'Test' and 'Train' phases, while the inner loop includes 'Val.' (Validation) and 'Train' phases. After training, 'Final Models' are developed. These models undergo 'Classification Evaluation', which includes a graph showing 'True Positive Rate' versus 'False Positive Rate' with an AUC of 0.75 and a 'SHAP Feature Importance' bar chart displaying features A to F with their respective mean SHAP values.
Flowchart of protein classification model workflow with dataset creation and splitting, model training, testing and evaluation steps.

Direct PC modeling aims to predict the identity and/or abundance of proteins adsorbed onto NP surfaces (Tables S2 and S3). Model outputs may include binary predictions of protein binding, enrichment versus depletion classifications, or quantitative estimates of protein abundance within the PC. These predictions enable in silico screening of candidate NPs designs before experimental testing. Most direct PC models treat adsorption as binary classification tasks (e.g., protein presence or absence). Consistently, ensemble tree-based methods, particularly Random Forest Classifiers (RFC), outperform linear or distance-based approaches like SVM, Logistic Regression (LR), and k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) and have even been found to outperform more complex architectures like Deep Neural Networks (DNN) for classification tasks. Reported performance generally falls within AUC values of ~0.75 to 0.97, or F1-scores between ~0.6 to 0.85, demonstrating moderate to excellent prediction accuracy. In a representative workflow, Canchola et al. used nested CV to train several classification models to predict whether key cellular internalization-related proteins (APOE, APOB-100, CO3, and CLUS) would be present, using NP physicochemical properties and incubation-related experimental parameters as inputs (Figure 3). Analysis of model results and feature importance highlights NP characteristics strongly associated with protein presence, pointing to design features that could be tuned in subsequent experimental validation studies.

Regression-based direct PC prediction models have also been developed to predict quantitative protein abundance. Random Forest Regression (RFR) is similarly the most commonly employed algorithm. Reported regression performance is generally more variable than classification, falling within R2 values of ~0.53 to ~0.82, depending on the modeling framework and feature representation. Regression tasks present additional challenges compared to binary adsorption classification as quantitative proteomics measurements reflect not only adsorption affinity but also the initial abundance of proteins within the incubation medium, the ability of the model to handle zero and near-zero values, and other experimental considerations.

Two recent studies applied RFR to the same integrated NP-PC dataset (originally curated by Ban et al.) to predict protein abundance, yet reported different predictive performance and feature importance patterns. While Liao et al. reported moderate predictive performance (average R2 = 0.68, RMSE = 0.90) across 60 selected proteins, Fu et al. reported lower predictive performance for their model (average R2 = 0.534, RMSE = 0.760) across a larger subset of 178 selected proteins. Furthermore, while features like incubation plasma concentration, nanoparticle polydispersity index (PDI), and surface modification were key in Liao et al.’s model, Fu et al. highlighted experimental variables such as protein source and centrifugation conditions, as well as NP composition, as important contributors to their model predictions. While exact differences in modeling strategies (such as model hyperparameters or CV strategies) cannot be fully evaluated due to incomplete code reporting, several methodological differences likely contributed to discrepancies. First, the studies applied different data preprocessing and splitting strategies: Liao et al. used a 90/10 train-test split to their data and applied Random Oversampling to address the high prevalence of zero values in the regression target distribution, whereas Fu et al used an 80/20 split without resampling. Oversampling is a technique that can increase the representation of underrepresented outcomes during training to potentially improve fit, but also risks increased sensitivity to noise, affecting absolute error metrics such as Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE). In addition, while Liao et al. reported Gini importance for feature importance analysis, Fu et al. utilized SHAP values. Because these approaches quantify importance through fundamentally different mechanisms, they can produce different rankings of influential variables even when applied to similar dataset and model type. Together, these studies illustrate how modeling decisions can substantially affect both reported performance and inferred mechanistic drivers, even when analyses are conducted on the same underlying dataset.
Predicting NP-protein interaction properties

While Section 3.1 focuses on prediction the specific composition of adsorbed proteins, other computational approaches instead predict interaction-level properties such as NP-protein binding affinities, adsorption energies, or protein functional or physicochemical properties (Table S4 and S5).These models aim to capture the underlying molecular determinants of PC formation rather than the full ensemble of adsorbed proteins and their relative abundances. Such indirect modeling is especially useful when the goal is to understand interaction mechanisms or design generalizable principles, rather than predict exact PC compositions for a specific NP formulation.

Ban et al. trained an RFR to predict abundance of proteins with particular overall physicochemical and functional properties of the PC. In this way, the authors modeled not the abundance of specific proteins in the PC, but 1) the percentage that immune, apolipoprotein, clusterin, complement, or coagulation proteins would comprise the PC of a given NP formulation, or 2) the percentage of the PC that would be comprised of proteins of a certain MW, pI, GRAVY range, etc. Initial analysis using conventional linear regression resulted in weak relationships (R2 <0.4), which the authors attributed to the high heterogeneity of their dataset that resulted in overrepresentation of certain NP-protein combinations and underrepresentation of others. In particular, non-inorganic NPs such as lipid and liposomal NPs. The RFR models, however, were found to better capture nonlinear relationships among NP properties, experimental conditions, and protein characteristics (average R2 >0.5). Similarly, Yan et al. used a deep learning, image-based CNN framework to predict the overall degree of protein adsorption on various NP formulations to indicate an NPs general ability to interact with biomolecules rather than individual protein adsorption values.

The GraphBNC model framework developed by Pihlajamäki et al. for predicting NP-protein interactions. This figure has been reproduced under the terms of the Creative Commons CC BY license from © 2024 Pihlajamäki et al. Advanced Materials published by Wiley-VCH GmbH.

It begins with initialization, showing protein and AuNC structures. Protein and AuNC featurization involves creating a protein graph and AuNC representation, extracting residue features through WL updates and determining global and local features. Paired features are used for NN interaction predictions with a three-hidden-layer feedforward NN. Predicted interaction energy contributions are collected as E subscript Coul and E subscript vdW, with scaling from weak to strong. Detection of coarse-grained interaction sites includes initialization, simulated annealing, agglomerative clustering and prediction of coarse-grained complexes, highlighting top interaction sites. The final complex shows refined all-atom positions.
Diagram of protein and AuNC interaction prediction process with initialization, feature extraction and final complex refinement.

ML-based frameworks have also been developed to predict adsorption energies, and interaction strength for analysis of binding stability and optimal NP binding sites. In their approach, Pihlajamäki et al. developed GraphBNC, a graph-based NN model that integrates protein structure and gold NP ligand features to predict the contributions of Coulomb and van der Waals interactions to NP-protein interaction energy. The predicted interaction energies were then coupled with a Monte Carlo-based simulated annealing scheme to statistically identify the most stable binding sites on protein surfaces and their estimated interaction times (Figure 4). Wyrzykowska et al. and Saeedimasine et al. respectively used quantitative structure-property relationship (QSPR) and quantitative structure-activity relationship (QSAR) frameworks to independently adsorption energies between NPs and protein residues. Analyses identified several key NP and protein properties that drove NP-protein interactions, including protein MW and surface area, the ability of an NP to interact electrostatically with a biomolecule, and an NP’s quadrupole and dipole moments.

Beyond predictive modeling, unsupervised dimensionality techniques can also provide insight into global NP-protein interaction patterns. Prior studies have applied Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP), k-Means Clustering, or t-Distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) to analyze high-dimensional proteomic or spectroscopic datasets. These approaches use metric-based embeddings and manifold learning to reduce complex, high-dimensional data into low-dimensional representations that retain the underlying patterns and structural relationships of the original dataset. Thus, these unsupervised ML methods can complement predictive models beyond simple dimensionality reduction during feature processing by identifying common interaction regimes and global structure within complex NP-PC datasets.
Overall trends in PC modeling frameworks

Both direct and indirect modeling strategies have been used to characterize PC formation and NP-protein interactions. However, methodological practices across studies remain heterogeneous (Tables S3 and S5). Only 27% of models (n = 6) incorporate robust splitting strategies like stratified train-test partitioning to minimize training bias. Notably, study-aware data partitioning to account for cross-study batch effects was not implemented in any of the models, despite the increasing use of curated, multi-study datasets. After splitting, many of the investigated PC datasets for classification exhibited class imbalance, with substantially more binding proteins than non-binding proteins. To address this imbalance, resampling strategies, such as Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) or its regression-variant, SmoteR was incorporated into 6 model frameworks to manage sparse or zero-heavy outcomes. SMOTE generates synthetic samples for the minority (i.e., less frequent) class in the training set by interpolating between existing observations in feature space, reducing bias toward the majority class while preserving information from the original data. However, because it assumes that interpolated samples represent plausible observations, it can introduce artificial structure in small or noisy datasets, potentially amplifying existing biases rather than correcting them. As a result, efficacy of such approaches depends heavily on dataset size, feature space structure, and the underlying experimental design.

Training and validation practices across studies are similarly varied. Hyperparameter optimizations and model training most commonly used 5 to 10-fold CV using the GridSearchCV Function (23%; n = 5). GridSearchCV evaluates predefined combinations of parameters using CV performance (e.g., prediction error or model score). However, it can become computationally inefficient for large search spaces common in high-dimensional data and complex models such as NNs, and may miss optimal configurations if the search space is not sufficiently detailed. Although GridSearchCV provides a simple framework for hyperparameter tuning, evaluating many configurations on the same CV folds can produce optimistically biased performance estimates. To mitigate this selection bias, recent implementation of Nested CV separates hyperparameter optimization from model evaluation, though its computational cost motivates more efficient search strategies such as Bayesian Optimization.

The diversity of model training strategies makes direct comparison of reported performance metrics challenging and underscores the need for consistent benchmarking practices across future PC prediction studies. Nearly half of models did not perform or did not report hyperparameter optimization methods (50%; n = 11) and/or full final hyperparameters used to train the models (41%; n = 9), making it difficult (or even impossible in some cases) to replicate results or incorporate models into workflows. Greater transparency and standardization in model tuning and evaluation will therefore be important for improving comparability and confidence of future PC prediction models. These limitations and proposed strategies to address them are further discussed in Section 5.3: Model Development and Framework Reporting and Section 6: Future Perspectives.

Despite dataset and framework variations, feature importance analyses consistently identify protein-related descriptors, especially pI, MW, amino acid content, and hydrophobicity, as well as experimental properties related to media protein content consistently emerge as dominant predictors of NP-PC binding. These trends are consistent with experimental observation and fundamental adsorption mechanisms and support the perspective that PC formation is a process governed by biochemical compatibility and solution dynamics. Inclusion of these features in future models shows great potential to improve model performance and enhance translational relevance of their predictions.
Pc as descriptors for biomedical applications

ML-driven approaches have also begun to leverage the PC as descriptors for modeling nano-bio interactions, building on the demonstrated importance of protein characteristics in pharmacological experiments and NP-PC formation modeling. Despite growing evidence of their use as mechanistically meaningful descriptors and recent advances in ML algorithms, few existing models were found in our literature search that explicitly incorporate PC information into their frameworks; instead, the majority of existing models focus solely on NP physicochemical descriptors and experimental conditions, which may constrain their ability to predict PC-bio interactions. Table S6 and S7 lists specific studies, associated endpoints, and model development strategies.
Cell association

Cell association is the most common target for models incorporating PC profiles, as NP and NP-PC complex interactions between membranes and/or surface receptors strongly influence pharmacological and toxicological outcomes. In drug delivery, cell association is a key step as NPs must be internalized through phagocytic, pinocytic, and receptor-mediated endocytic mechanisms. Experimental studies have shown that PC composition can alter NP bioactivity, with certain proteins enhancing or inhibiting membrane association with target cells or tissues. Several regression-based models have been developed to predict cell association based on NP properties, PC profiles, and additional protein features (e.g., overall protein density, refractive index sensitivity). Interestingly, although physicochemical and electrostatic properties of proteins like charge or binding motifs often directly mediate cell interactions, no existing models incorporated these characteristics as input features.

Walkey et al. linked NP physicochemical properties, human-serum PC profiles, and association with human lung epithelial carcinoma (A549) cells. The study compared simple single-protein LR models with a multivariate partial least-squares regression (PLSR) model using the full PC fingerprint, demonstrating substantially improved predictive accuracy with the multivariate approach (PSLR: Q2 ≈0.81 vs. LR: Q2 ≈0.44). Subsequent studies using the same dataset consistently report substantial accuracy gains when PC features were included compared to NP property-only models. Feature ranking analyses consistently indicated the presence of inter-alpha trypsin inhibitor heavy chain H2, complement C3, and alpha-1-antitrypsin proteins as strong predictors for the enhancement or inhibition of cell association. When integrated with models capable of predicting individual protein binding, such approaches could support multiscale frameworks linking protein corona composition to downstream cellular outcomes. Although NP-cell association is often used as a proxy for endpoints such as NP internalization and toxicity, no studies were identified that directly model NP cellular uptake using protein corona descriptors, revealing a notable gap in the current literature on NP drug delivery efficiency.
Biodistribution & delivery efficiency

Workflow of the feedforward neural network with backpropagation used to predict NP accumulation in rat organs developed by Lazarovits et al.. Created in BioRender. Canchola, A. (2026) https://BioRender.com/3d8s18h.

It begins with input data labeled as '707 PC LFQ Intensities' and target output labeled as 'NP Organ Accumulation'. These inputs are fed into the neural network, which undergoes 375 iterations. The network outputs predicted accumulation, which is compared to true accumulation. The error is calculated using a loss function labeled 'Calculate Error (MSE)'. Error backpropagation is used to adjust weights, refining the model. The process results in a final model for predicting nanoparticle accumulation.
Flowchart of neural network predicting NP accumulation in rat organs using input data and backpropagation.

Biodistribution and delivery efficiency of NPs are important for evaluating the safety and organ-specific efficacy of drug delivery systems. Only one study was found that directly incorporated PC profiles into biodistribution modeling. This study, Lazarovits et al., incorporated quantitative PC profiles alongside NP properties as input features for an NN model to predict the in vivo half-life, spleen accumulation, and liver accumulation (all endpoint accuracies >75%) of gold NP dispersions in mouse models (Figure 5). This gap in research may stem from experimental challenges associated with validating in vivo biodistribution and PC formation, as relatively few studies have systematically characterized PCs formed in in vivo conditions or directly compared in vitro and in vivo PC profiles. However, we are not able to find existing examples of such an approach at the time of this review.
Biomarker detection for disease diagnosis

Several groups have also highlighted the use of NP-PC profiles for traditional ML (largely RFC and LR) and deep learning-based detection of diseases including cancers and cardiovascular conditions. Guha et al., for example, developed binary and multiclass LR models that incorporated PC profiles from NPs incubated in patient samples to distinguish between healthy patients and patients with atherosclerotic vascular disease (ASCVD) and/or metastatic prostate cancer. Notably, their framework went beyond standard predictive modeling by incorporating actual causality inference (using counterfactual reasoning) to identify proteins with potential causal relationships. First, LR with LASSO regularization-based feature selection, identified 22 proteins strongly correlated with diseased states. Then, actual causality analysis was applied on this protein subset to evaluate whether the absence of a given protein would eliminate the observed disease outcome under otherwise identical conditions. Using this two-stage approach, the authors identified TBXAS1, actin beta, and eukaryotic translation initiation factor 5A-1 as potential causal drivers of ASCVD, with TBXAS1 also implicated in metastatic prostate cancer.

Another example by Rakhshandeh et al. applied both traditional models and more advanced NN models to classify PCs formed on gold NPs associated with healthy patients or patients with choroidal melanoma. In contrast to studies that used PC abundance values from high-resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS), this work calculated statistical and entropy-based features from raw proteomics data, and converted 1D mass spectra to 2D image representations using Continuous Wavelet Transforms (CWT). These models achieved exceptional classification accuracy, but required more extensive computational resources and time-intensive preprocessing steps, reflecting a trade-off between model performance and practical scalability.
In vitro/in vivo toxicity modeling

Prior experimental studies have shown that PC profiles may alter inflammasome profiles, influence oxidative stress responses, or activate various cell death pathways. ML-based approaches to predict adverse outcomes of NPs are widespread in the literature to predict endpoints like cell viability, NAEOL/LAEOL values, immune toxicity, EC50 values, and more. These endpoints have been widely modeled based on structural, atomic-based, and physicochemical NP properties, but studies directly incorporating PC profiles or PC-derived structural or physicochemical features are limited in our literature search. We found one particular study, Li et al., that combined PC-derived features with Surface Enhanced Raman Scattering (SERS) profiling of cellular secretomes for pristine and aged (UV irradiated) polystyrene nanoplastics. Using t-SNE analysis, they identified distinct clustering patterns that reflected different cell death pathways. Exposure to aged nanoparticles shifted cell fate toward ferroptosis, while PC adsorption was found to modulate this effect, producing a mixture of apoptosis, necrosis, and ferroptosis signatures. Additional indirect evidence supporting the importance of PC effects comes from models that incorporate protein-related experimental conditions. Martin et al. trained a suite of NNs, linear, tree-, and kernel-based models to predict cytotoxicity of silica NPs using physicochemical descriptors and environmental attributes such as cell culture medium composition and serum concentration. Although PC features were not explicitly included, SHAP analysis identified serum-related variables as among the most influential predictors of toxicity. Removing the serum feature substantially reduced model performance, suggesting the importance of NP-PC interactions in nanotoxicity prediction.
Other modeling applications for NP optimization

An additional example highlighting the use of PC descriptors in predictive nanomaterial modeling for NP properties was reported by Sengottiyan et al., who developed a PC-informed nano-QSPR framework to model the ζ-potential of polymeric NP. The resulting genetic algorithm-partial least squares (GA-PLS) model demonstrated strong predictive performance (R2 = 0.96; Q2 EXT = 0.89), providing proof-of-concept that NP electrokinetic properties are determined not only by intrinsic NP structure but also by extrinsic factors arising from PC formation in biological media. This work highlights the importance of integrating both nanomaterial descriptors and PC-derived features when developing predictive models of NP behavior, particularly for properties linked to stability, cellular interactions, and potential toxicity. Because electrokinetic properties such as ζ-potential play an important role in governing nanoparticle colloidal stability, changes in protein adsorption can also indirectly influence aggregation behavior and effective particle size in biological environments, further impacting downstream biological responses.
Overall trends in PC-Informed biomedical frameworks

Although the number of ML studies that explicitly incorporate protein corona (PC) information into biomedical prediction tasks remains limited, several methodological patterns emerge across the available literature (Table S6).

A defining characteristic of these studies is the imbalance between dataset dimensionality and sample size. Many models incorporate high-dimensional PC descriptors derived from proteomic measurements, where protein abundance features may be available for only a few dozen NP samples. For example, cell association studies were largely conduced on datasets of fewer than 150 NP samples. This is particularly true for biomarker analysis studies that used datasets derived from PCs formed in human plasma and serum from healthy and diseased donors. In some cases, datasets contained as few as 12 samples (6 samples per class). Wang et al. reported the largest dataset (n = 297) for their multi-class RFC models, but effective sample sizes per disease class remained relatively small (average ≈50).

Limited sample sizes and high feature-to-sample ratios reduce the statistical power available for training robust models and increase the likelihood that algorithms learn dataset-specific noise rather than generalizable biological patterns. As a result, models developed on small cohorts or limited NP samples may perform well during internal validation but fail to maintain predictive accuracy when applied to independent datasets or patient populations. Unlike we observed with models in Section 3, resampling was not a reported strategy in any of the biomedical models. While this does pose a major challenge, feature selection or dimensionality reduction strategies can help preserve meaningful patterns in the data while minimizing noise and reducing model complexity. Approaches such as correlation-based filtering, recursive feature elimination, or regularization techniques were used in nearly all identified studies to identify features (often proteins) most strongly associated with the biological endpoint. Helma et al. addressed this issue in their cell association model by applying Pearson correlation-based feature filtering (p < 0.05) to remove irrelevant descriptors prior to similarity calculations, and by constructing local QSAR models based on subsets of similar nanoparticles, analogous to a k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) strategy.

Validation practices also reflect the constraints of the small dataset. Most studies, particularly QSAR models, employ leave-one-out (LOO) or leave-many-out (LMO) CV schemes (36%, n = 5), while others maximize the full dataset by evaluating performance through the stability of metrics such as R2 and RMSE across folds instead of evaluating on a fixed test set. Although strategies such as feature engineering and data augmentation can partially address limited datasets and help identify trends within the training data, they cannot fully substitute for large, standardized datasets (see Section 5.1: Data Diversity and Underrepresentation of NP types). Nonetheless, translation is possible: Fan et al. successfully validated their NP-PC diagnostic model using an independent external cohort of clinical serum samples (n = 39) and observed strong agreement between internal and external validation performance, with diagnostic accuracy approaching 95%.

These findings demonstrate that robust generalization is achievable, but also highlight the importance of larger cohorts and external validation when developing clinically relevant PC-based diagnostic models. At the same time, ML approaches can still enable meaningful analysis of limited datasets by facilitating feature selection and identifying informative protein signatures in otherwise high-dimensional data, as demonstrated by the LASSO-based dimensionality reduction and subsequent causal analysis in Guha et al.
Challenges & limitations of prediction models

Despite the major advances in ML modeling for nanomedicine discussed in this review, several key challenges remain in the current ML-PC landscape. These limitations arise from three factors: limited diversity and representativeness of available datasets, variability in experimental workflows used to generate protein corona measurements, and insufficient transparency in model development and reporting practices. In this section, we discuss these limitations in detail and emphasize considerations that must be addressed to improve future modeling efforts.
Data diversity and underrepresentation of NP types

The most pertinent issue is the limited diversity of NP systems represented in available datasets. First, NP formulations investigated in PC studies are highly homogeneous. Prior reviews have documented that despite the growing interest in the clinical application of organic NPs, such as lipid NPs, liposomes, polymeric micelles, extracellular vesicles (EV), protein-based NPs, and nucleic-acid nanostructures, studies investigating their PC profiles and formation mechanisms are vastly underrepresented in both experimental and ML modeling efforts. Instead, the literature is dominated by spherical inorganic NPs, typically comprised of metal or metal oxide cores (e.g., silver, gold, and iron oxide), often with similar surface characteristics. Of the models reviewed here, only 6 incorporated lipid- or DNA-based NP types to model PC composition and none incorporated EVs or protein-based NPs. All models for biomedical applications focused solely on metal, metal oxide, silica, and/or polymer (polystyrene) NPs. Consequently, while the reported models can reveal important trends within their training sets, it remains uncertain whether they can reliably be extrapolated to clinically relevant nanomedicine platforms.

This limitation is likely significant, as the physicochemical properties of many organic NPs differ fundamentally from more rigid inorganic NPs. For example, nucleic-acid nanostructures (e.g., DNA or mRNA) form complex 3D architectures whose sequence-dependent electrostatic and steric properties may influence PC adsorption mechanisms and kinetics in ways not captured by models trained primarily on inorganic NP descriptors. Among the studies that incorporate more clinically relevant NPs, Huzar et al. was the only study to explicitly incorporate descriptors specific to DNA nanostructures; however, the features used largely captured global structural characteristics (e.g., volume, surface area, and number of vertices) rather than sequence- or nucleotide-level properties, such as base composition or local electrostatic features. Evidence of generalization challenges have already been observed even among relatively similar inorganic NP systems. Walkey et al. saw poor prediction accuracy when attempting to validate their models, trained with gold NP-derived features, with silver NPs. Even both materials were included in the training dataset, the resulting linear models showed limited cross-material transferability for their linear models. To our knowledge, systematic external or experimental validation of models trained on inorganic NP datasets for predicting PC behavior on organic NPs has not been performed.

Second, the majority of PC profiles currently used for modeling are derived from in vitro experiments, typically performed using plasma or serum obtained from healthy human or murine donors. Canchola et al. reported that less than 4% of entries in their curated PC-DB were derived from in vivo studies, and that all such data originated from mouse or rat systems with no human in vivo data represented. As the composition and behavior of the NP-PC complex in living organisms are influenced by complex physiological factors such as plasma protein heterogeneity or blood flow dynamics, models trained exclusively on in vitro data may fail to accurately predict PC formation or related biological responses. This limitation is particularly critical given that many NP systems are ultimately intended for administration in patients with underlying diseases, where the protein composition of biological fluids can differ substantially from those extracted from healthy donors. Studies using PC profiles for disease detection (Section 4.4) provide direct evidence that PCs formed in samples from diseased patients can differ systematically from those formed in healthy controls, reflecting disease-associated changes in the plasma proteome. Thus, predictive models trained only on healthy donor samples may be learning largely irrelevant protein adsorption patterns and may not be directly translatable to clinically relevant applications.

Finally, while this review primarily focuses on the PC corona, it is increasingly recognized that NP can acquire a broader biomolecular corona composed not only of proteins but also lipids, metabolites, nucleic acids, and other biomolecules that were underreported in our literature search. Incorporating these additional biomolecular components into future experimental datasets and ML modeling frameworks may further improve the biological realism and predictive capability of nano-bio interaction models.
Experimental variability and reporting

In addition to limitations in dataset diversity, variability in experimental design and reporting further complicates the development of reliable predictive models. Because PC formation experiments vary widely in NP preparation, biological matrices, incubation conditions, and analytical workflows, resulting datasets may reflect both genuine biological interactions and laboratory-specific artifacts. These inconsistencies can directly affect ML model development, as models trained on heterogeneous or poorly described datasets may learn dataset-specific biases rather than generalizable nano-bio interaction patterns.

Inter-laboratory and operator-dependent variability can further complicate interpretation and accurate modeling. Typical analytical workflows include NP preparation, incubation with biological fluids, washing or separation of NP-PC complexes, and proteomic analysis. Differences in sample preparation, centrifugation or washing, NP dosing, and instrument calibration procedures can all influence the measured corona composition. Moreover, environmental factors such as ionic strength, protein concentration, incubation time, and biological matrix composition are known to affect NP-PC adsorption dynamics. As a result, PC profiles obtained under different laboratory conditions may reflect both genuine biological effects and methodological variability. Furthermore, most PC profiling studies rely on HRMS-based proteomics, which provides powerful tools for identifying and quantifying adsorbed proteins but is also susceptible to missing values. Low analyte ion abundance, background signal interference, instrumental variation, and data-processing steps can all lead to incomplete detection of proteins or underestimation of their abundance. Consequently, models that directly use raw or minimally processed proteomics features may inadvertently inherit measurement noise or systematic biases present in the experimental data. When experimental conditions and measurement methodologies are not consistently documented, algorithms may inadvertently learn patterns that reflect dataset-specific artifacts rather than true biological relationships governing NP-PC interactions.
Model development and framework reporting

Limitations in model development transparency and reporting practices can also hinder the reproducibility and reliability of ML-based PC prediction studies, even when using standardized, diverse datasets. In many of the studies reviewed here, annotated code and modeling workflows were provided either as supplementary material or through public repositories such as GitHub (41% of models had readily available code; n = 16). However, for studies modeling downstream biological endpoints, publicly accessible code and complete modeling pipelines were rarely available. In some cases, repository links were provided but were no longer accessible, further limiting reproducibility. Even when code was available, improper or incomplete documentation is extremely common, resulting in a high barrier of entry for non-computational researchers when attempting to interpret or validate models. The absence of accessible code and documentation makes it difficult to evaluate or replicate the modeling procedures used in these studies.

Incomplete reporting of modeling workflows, especially for those that do not provide code, also obscures key methodological details that are essential for evaluating model robustness. For example, many studies state that CV was performed but provide minimal information beyond reporting that “k-fold CV” (e.g., 4-fold or 10-fold) was used. Without specifying the CV strategy (e.g., such as whether folds were stratified, grouped by experimental batch, repeated, etc.) it becomes difficult to assess whether model performance estimates may have been influenced by data leakage or sampling bias. Similarly, details regarding hyperparameter optimization, feature selection procedures, and preprocessing steps are often underreported (39% reported full final hyperparameters used for model training, n = 15). In the absence of such information, it is not always clear whether hyperparameters were systematically optimized or manually selected, how feature selection was implemented relative to CV folds, or whether preprocessing transformations were applied in a leakage-free manner. These ambiguities can significantly affect model performance and generalizability, making it challenging for readers to assess the rigor of the reported modeling frameworks.

These issues are particularly important given the rapid growth of accessible ML tools and programming assistants. The emergence of large language models (LLMs) and coding agents has lowered the barrier to implementing ML models for researchers without extensive computational backgrounds. This increased accessibility is beneficial for accelerating interdisciplinary research in nanomedicine, but also underscores the importance of transparent reporting and code availability. Insufficient documentation makes it difficult to perform quality assurance, reproduce analyses, or externally validate published models. In some cases, the lack of methodological transparency prevents independent researchers from reconstructing the modeling pipeline altogether, effectively limiting external validation and hindering progress toward robust predictive frameworks.
Future perspectives
Key challenges in the reviewed NP-PC modeling landscape, their impact of ML-PC readiness, and potential solutions.

Addressing the challenges outlined above will be essential for advancing ML-driven modeling of protein corona formation and its biological consequences. Several developments in experimental design, data infrastructure, and computational methodology may substantially improve the robustness and translational relevance of future models (Table 2). Future progress in this field will depend on coordinated advances in both experimental and computational practices.

First, to improve the biological relevance of future models, increased experimental efforts using diverse, clinically relevant NP designs, biological matrices, and exposure conditions should be prioritized. External validation across independent datasets, laboratories, and nanoparticle systems can also play a critical role in identifying these limitations. By testing models on data generated under different experimental conditions or laboratories or using previously unseen clinically relevant NP types like lipid or liposomal systems, researchers can more readily detect failures in model generalization and identify gaps in training datasets. In this vein, systematic external validation can serve as a benchmark of model performance and as a practical mechanism for identifying dataset biases and guiding the development of more representative training datasets.

Improving experimental reproducibility and data interoperability will also play an important role in advancing ML-based PC modeling. Recent work has emphasized the need for standardized experimental protocols and transparent reporting practices for PC studies. In a cross-facility investigation, Ashkarran et al. demonstrated that differences in sample preparation workflows, LC-MS instrumentation, database search parameters, and downstream data-processing pipelines can lead to substantial discrepancies in proteome coverage and protein detection across laboratories. They emphasized that stepwise harmonization of analytical procedures including standardized sample preparation, consistent instrumentation settings, and unified database search and data-processing workflows significantly increased the proportion of shared proteins detected across facilities. Even partial standardization, such as applying a common data-processing pipeline, markedly improved dataset homogeneity. Complementary perspectives from Tang et al. further highlight the importance of comprehensive reporting of experimental methodologies and suggest that automated experimental pipelines may help reduce operator-dependent variability and batch effects in large-cohort proteomics studies.

Broader adoption of FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, and Reusable) data practices will also be essential for improving the usability of future PC datasets for ML applications. FAIR principles emphasize standardized metadata annotation, transparent documentation of experimental workflows, and accessible data sharing formats. This would involve consistent reporting of nanoparticle physicochemical descriptors, incubation conditions, biological matrix characteristics, and proteomics processing parameters. Such structured metadata would enable datasets generated by different laboratories to be more readily aggregated and analyzed using ML models. These efforts are important for ensuring that future PC datasets are “ML ready,” allowing models trained across laboratories and experimental platforms to produce reproducible and transferable predictions.

In parallel with expanded experimental coverage and improved experimental standardization in future studies, several best practices should be adopted for those developing and reporting ML models. Study-aware or stratified CV schemes, as discussed in Section 2, can ensure that samples originating from the same laboratory or experimental batch are grouped together during model training and evaluation, reducing the risk of inflated performance due to shared experimental artifacts. Additional approaches, such as batch-effect correction and normalization methods, can further help account for systematic differences between datasets. While these computational approaches cannot fully substitute for well-controlled experimental data, they can improve model robustness when working with heterogeneous datasets derived from multiple studies. In addition, future model approaches should prioritize complete, interpretable, and standardized reporting practices. These include providing publicly accessible code and trained models whenever possible, clearly documenting preprocessing workflows, feature selection procedures, and hyperparameter optimization strategies, and explicitly describing the CV framework used for model evaluation. Reporting should also specify dataset partitioning strategies, including whether stratified or study-aware splits were used to account for batch effects or experimental grouping. Where feasible, authors should provide versioned repositories and archived code snapshots to ensure long-term accessibility. Adopting these practices will improve transparency, enable independent validation of reported results, and support the development of more reliable and generalizable ML models for predicting NP-PC interactions and associated biological responses. Emerging guidelines for ML reporting in biomedical research, such as TRIPOD-AI or MINIMAR-style standards, may provide useful frameworks for improving transparency in future PC modeling efforts. MINIMAR in particular emphasizes clear and detailed reporting of information related to model optimization and evaluation strategies, input features and output targets, evaluation metrics, hyperparameter chosen, and publishing of training data and code. While training data may not always be able to be published due to privacy concerns (particularly those using human samples), broader reporting model development and evaluation will greatly improve transparency and confidence in model performance and transferability.
Toward end-to-end ML-Guided nanoparticle optimization
Design Space Generation: Generate candidate nanoparticle designs with varying physicochemical properties.
PC Composition Prediction: Apply ML models trained on NP-PC interaction datasets to predict the PC composition or ideal interaction properties for each candidate NP.
Biological Outcome Prediction: Use PC-informed models to estimate downstream endpoints such as cellular uptake, immune activation, or biodistribution.
Design Optimization: Apply optimization algorithms to identify nanoparticle designs predicted to maximize desired outcomes while minimizing adverse effects.

Beyond predictive modeling, machine learning frameworks have the potential to support end-to-end optimization of nanoparticle design by integrating predictions across multiple stages of nano– bio interaction. In such a workflow, models predicting protein corona composition could first be used to estimate the set of proteins likely to adsorb to a candidate nanoparticle formulation under specific biological conditions. These predicted PC profiles could then serve as input features for downstream models that predict biological outcomes such as cellular uptake, immune recognition, biodistribution, or toxicity. By linking these models within a unified framework, it becomes possible to simulate how changes in NP physicochemical properties propagate through PC formation and ultimately influence biological responses. For example, an optimization workflow might proceed as follows:

An integrated framework could substantially accelerate nanoparticle development by enabling rapid in silico screening of large design spaces prior to experimental testing. As more comprehensive datasets linking nanoparticle properties, protein corona composition, and biological outcomes become available, these multiscale modeling strategies may ultimately support the rational design of nanomedicine platforms optimized for specific therapeutic applications.
Conclusions

The PC plays a critical role in shaping the biological behavior of NPs, influencing pharmacokinetics, cellular interactions, toxicity, and overall therapeutic efficacy. Despite extensive characterization, the complexity of NP-PC interactions has made it challenging to comprehensively predict PC formation and its downstream effects. ML approaches have emerged as powerful tools to address these challenges, enabling the prediction of PC composition, NP-protein adsorption dynamics, and downstream biological behavior of NPs. However, limited dataset diversity and clinical relevance, variability in experimental workflows, and insufficient transparency in model development and reporting procedures currently restrict their [their translation from data-driven analyses to clinically applicable tools. Addressing these challenges through expanded datasets, improved experimental standardization, FAIR data practices, and transparent modeling frameworks will be critical for advancing the field. As datasets become more diverse and modeling frameworks more robust, ML-driven approaches may increasingly support integrated workflows linking nanoparticle design, protein corona prediction, and biological outcome modeling. Such developments have the potential to bridge experimental nanomedicine and data-driven modeling, ultimately enabling the development of safer and more effective nanomedicine technologies.

Article highlights
Machine learning models can predict protein adsorption patterns and downstream biological responses.
Dataset limitations, workflow variability, and insufficient reporting standards remain key barriers.
Improved data diversity, experimental standardization, and transparent modeling are required.
Integrated predictive workflows may bridge experimental nanomedicine with data-driven nanoparticle design.

The article illustrates how curated databases combined with ML can enable predictive insights into NP-PC interactions and cellular recognition.

infons.note: •• This article demonstrate the use of PC profiles combined with ML to predict biomarkers for disease diagnosis. The study highlights how feature importance can reduce high-dimensional noise and guide subsequent causality analyses, illustrating the value of ML for extracting meaningful signals from complex nanomedicine datasets.

infons.note: •• This article examines sources of variability in NP-PC characterization across laboratories, identifying key analytical steps that contribute to inconsistent results. The study provides practical recommendations for standardizing protocols to improve reproducibility and data comparability in nanomedicine research.

infons.note: •• Article presents a framework outlining the minimum information that should be reported in medical AI studies, including model architecture, feature processing, and code transparency. These guidelines provide a useful template for improving reproducibility and reporting standards in future ML-driven nanomedicine research.
